attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with her fourth chest infection this year. She has a medical history of diffuse large B-cell lymphoma treated with rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine and prednisolone (R-CHOP) chemotherapy followed by autologous stem cell transplantation two years ago and is currently in remission. Her other medical conditions include osteoporosis. Her medications include calcium supplements and vitamin D.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Chest examination reveals scattered crackles in the right lower zone. Recent blood tests show:

  • Haemoglobin 132 g/L (115-165)
  • White cell count 6.8 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)
  • Neutrophils 4.2 x 10^9/L (2.0-7.5)
  • Lymphocytes 1.8 x 10^9/L (1.0-4.0)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with recurrent respiratory infections following treatment for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. Secondary antibody deficiency is a well-recognised complication of B-cell depleting therapy (e.g., rituximab), stem cell, and solid organ transplantation.

Serum immunoglobulins are the most appropriate first-line investigation as they provide a rapid assessment of antibody deficiency. Low levels would indicate the need for immunoglobulin replacement therapy.

Lymphocyte subsets would be less helpful as the initial investigation, particularly given the normal lymphocyte count. While lymphocyte subsets can be useful in assessing B cell depletion after rituximab or in cases of lymphopenia, this patient's normal lymphocyte count makes this investigation less pressing than directly measuring antibody levels.

Immunoglobulin G subclasses testing is generally only useful after finding normal total IgG levels in patients with recurrent infections. It may also be measured when IgG4-related disease is suspected, regardless of total IgG levels. However, in this case of likely secondary antibody deficiency, it would not be the first-line investigation.

Pneumococcal vaccination response testing is used to assess specific antibody production and would typically be performed after finding borderline or low normal immunoglobulin levels. It is primarily used in the investigation of inborn errors of immunity rather than secondary hypogammaglobulinaemia. It is not an appropriate first-line test.

Peripheral blood flow cytometry may be useful in the evaluation of some lymphoproliferative disorders (e.g., chronic lymphocytic leukaemia, mantle cell leukaemia). However, it is not useful in this case for the evaluation of recurrent infections in this case.
learningPoints: Secondary antibody deficiency is a well-recognised complication of B-cell depleting therapy (e.g., rituximab), stem cell, and solid organ transplantation.
Serum immunoglobulin levels are the first-line investigation for suspected antibody deficiency.
references: Otani IM, Lehman HK, Jongco AM, et al. Practical guidance for the diagnosis and management of secondary hypogammaglobulinemia: A Work Group Report of the AAAAI Primary Immunodeficiency and Altered Immune Response Committees. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2022;149(5):1525-1560. doi:10.1016/j.jaci.2022.01.025
